content.text: Commit Type Taxonomy for GenAI Repos

attrs.alt: Conventional commit type taxonomy: standard types (feat, fix, refactor, docs, chore, test, ci) in the top row; GenAI-extended types (prompt, eval, safety, model, agent, perf) in the bottom row; a BREAKING CHANGE footer banner spanning the full width.

content.text: The diagram above separates the commit type set into two rows. The top row holds types that every software project recognises. The bottom row adds types that exist specifically because agentic AI systems have artefacts — prompts, evaluation suites, safety guardrails, model configurations, orchestration loops — that do not map cleanly onto any existing type.
Why Standard Conventional Commits Fall Short for AI Repos
The Conventional Commits specification defines a small set of types —
— designed around deterministic software where the relationship between a code change and its observable effect is straightforward. An agentic AI repo breaks that assumption in three important ways.
First, prompts are not code in any traditional sense. A 200-word edit to the system prompt can change the agent's behaviour more profoundly than adding 500 lines of business logic, yet
implies a user-visible product feature and
implies something inconsequential. Neither captures a deliberate behavioural edit to a prompt file.

Second, model upgrades and provider swaps are deployment events with significant risk. Swapping from one model version to another changes latency, cost, output style, and capability — none of which are visible in source code changes. A
commit is indistinguishable in a changelog from pinning a minor version of a formatting library.

Third, evaluation regressions are a special category of defect. When an eval run shows that the agent now answers 8% fewer questions correctly after a code change, that is not a bug in the traditional sense — no function throws an exception, no unit test fails. But it is a regression that must be traceable to a specific commit. Without an
type, these changes disappear into generic
, making it impossible to grep the log for all prompt changes without reading every commit body.
Changelog tooling treats them like a dependency bump when they are actually a significant operational event.
Adding 40 new test cases to the eval suite is not the same as adding a unit test; it updates the quality gate the entire team is measured against.
A guardrail added in response to a red-team finding deserves at least as much visibility as a
does not distinguish safety remediations from cosmetic bug fixes.
The Extended Type Set for GenAI Repos

The extended type set treats five additional areas as first-class commit types. Each maps to a distinct artefact category in an agentic AI repo, and each produces a distinct entry in automated changelogs and release notes.
— changes to any file that holds natural language instructions sent to a model: system prompts, user prompt templates, few-shot example files, chain-of-thought prefixes.
— additions to, removals from, or changes in the evaluation suite: new test cases, updated golden outputs, eval harness configuration, threshold adjustments.
— changes to guardrails, content filters, refusal logic, jailbreak mitigations, PII detection rules, or red-team-driven remediations.
— changes to which model is used, which version or snapshot is pinned, which provider supplies the inference, or which fine-tuned checkpoint is loaded.
— changes to the orchestration layer: the planning loop, tool-call routing, memory update logic, sub-agent hand-off protocol, or the wiring between tools and the LLM.

— changes motivated by latency, cost, or token-usage reduction: caching layers, batching, prompt compression, parallel tool calls. Used when the change does not alter observable behaviour, only resource consumption.
The following definitions are written for a shared team agreement. They are intentionally precise to prevent type ambiguity at review time.

— a new product capability that users or downstream systems can observe. Example: adding a file-upload tool to the agent that was not there before. Does not include internal refactors that happen to expose a new method.

— a correction to behaviour that was broken or wrong. Example: the agent was dropping the last message from the history array before each LLM call, causing context loss. A fix corrects it. Does not include eval regressions caught only by evals.

— any edit to a prompt file or template, regardless of size. A one-word change to the system prompt qualifies. Example: tightening the instruction that tells the agent to cite its sources in numbered footnotes.
— a change to the evaluation dataset, harness, or quality threshold. Example: adding 25 new question-answer pairs to the RAG accuracy benchmark, or raising the minimum pass rate from 80% to 85%.
— a change to any component that mitigates harm, enforces policy, or responds to a threat finding. Requires a
footer (see below). Example: adding a regex-based PII scrubber to the output filter after a red-team exercise.
— any change that affects which model or checkpoint runs in any environment. Example: pinning the staging environment to a new snapshot after a provider migration.

— changes to the orchestration loop, memory management, or tool-routing decisions that do not also change a visible user feature. Example: switching from sequential tool execution to parallel fan-out for independent tool calls.
— structural improvement with no change to externally observable behaviour or LLM output. Example: extracting prompt-formatting logic into a dedicated module.
— a change that reduces latency or cost without altering what users see. Example: adding a semantic cache in front of the embedding API to avoid duplicate calls.
— routine maintenance: dependency version bumps, build tool configuration, CI schedule changes. Nothing that a user, operator, or model would notice.
Conventional Commits allows an optional scope in parentheses after the type:
. Scopes are most useful when the repo contains multiple agents, multiple prompt files, or multiple tools, and readers need to filter changes by component without reading the body.
Recommended scope categories for agentic repos:
— when the repo has more than one agent. Examples:
— when the repo has a prompt registry with named files. Examples:
— when a commit touches the implementation or schema of a specific tool. Examples:
— for model or infrastructure changes scoped to one environment. Examples:
— when the repo runs separate eval suites per domain or capability. Examples:
Avoid overly granular scopes that require updating every time the file moves. The scope should be stable across renames. Prefer the logical component name over the file path.
The full template for a GenAI commit message adds optional structured footers below the conventional commit body. Standard footers (
) are extended with four new keys specific to AI systems.

<type>(<scope>): <short imperative description>

[optional body: what changed and why, wrapped at 72 characters]
[include behavioural impact if type is prompt, model, or agent]

[optional footers]
BREAKING CHANGE: <description of what breaks and migration path>
Prompt-Hash: sha256:<first-12-chars-of-sha256-of-prompt-file>
Eval-Run: <eval-run-id-or-url>
Safety-Reviewer: <github-handle>
Refs: #<issue-number>
Rules for the subject line: present-tense imperative ("add", "remove", "update", not "added" or "adding"); no period at the end; 72 characters maximum. The body is optional but required for
types — reviewers need to understand why the change was made, not just what changed.
ships with the standard type list. Extending it with the GenAI types requires a
that spreads the base config and adds the extra types to the allowlist.
Install and wire the linter via a
git hook. The hook runs after the commit message is written but before the commit object is created, so a violation aborts the commit with a clear error message.
Git Commit Hook for GenAI Repos
The following shell script installs as
(or via Husky / lefthook). It delegates to commitlint for type validation and additionally enforces that

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# .git/hooks/commit-msg   (or managed by Husky / lefthook)
# chmod +x .git/hooks/commit-msg

set -euo pipefail

COMMIT_MSG_FILE="$1"
COMMIT_MSG=$(cat "$COMMIT_MSG_FILE")

# 1. Run commitlint for type/scope/header validation
npx --no-install commitlint --edit "$COMMIT_MSG_FILE"

# 2. Extract the type from the first line
TYPE=$(echo "$COMMIT_MSG" | head -1 | sed 's/(.*//;s/:.*//')

# 3. Enforce body for high-signal types
BODY=$(echo "$COMMIT_MSG" | tail -n +3 | sed '/^$/d')

if [[ "$TYPE" =~ ^(safety|prompt|model)$ ]] && [[ -z "$BODY" ]]; then
  echo "ERROR: commit type '$TYPE' requires a body explaining the change."
  echo "  Prompt, model, and safety commits must describe the intent and"
  echo "  expected behavioural impact in the commit body."
  exit 1
fi

# 4. Enforce Safety-Reviewer footer for safety commits
if [[ "$TYPE" == "safety" ]]; then
  if ! grep -qE '^Safety-Reviewer: @?[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$' "$COMMIT_MSG_FILE"; then
    echo "ERROR: safety commits require a 'Safety-Reviewer: <handle>' footer."
    exit 1
  fi
fi

exit 0
Change Class Field: Behavioural vs Structural
Not all changes within a given type carry the same risk. A useful optional field in the commit body — or as a footer — is
, which distinguishes behavioural changes (those that can alter what the model outputs) from structural ones (those that reorganise code or configuration without touching the inference path).

— a change that can alter the model's outputs, even if only probabilistically. Any edit to a prompt, a model version swap, a change to tool schemas, a new guardrail, or a modification to temperature or sampling parameters. Requires eval evidence before merging.

— a change that reorganises the codebase, renames files, updates dependency pinning, or refactors the orchestration layer in a way that does not affect which tokens are sampled. Can merge after unit tests pass; does not require an eval run.
to a commit footer makes it possible to filter the git log for all commits that should have triggered an eval run, even months later during a post-incident review.
Prompt Hash Field in Commit Footers

Prompt files are text, and their content directly determines model behaviour. Including a cryptographic hash of the prompt file at commit time creates a tamper-evident link between a commit and the exact prompt that was in effect. This is useful when debugging a production issue: you can hash the file currently deployed and compare it against the commit-recorded hash to verify they are the same.

Prompt-Hash: sha256:3f4c8a91e2b7

# Generate the value with:
# sha256sum prompts/system.txt | cut -c1-12
# or on macOS:
# shasum -a 256 prompts/system.txt | cut -c1-12

Use only the first 12 characters of the hex digest. The full 64-character hash is unwieldy in a commit message; 12 characters provide enough entropy to be collision-resistant across the prompt corpus of any single project.
When a single commit touches multiple prompt files, record a hash for each:
Automate hash computation in the commit hook to eliminate human error.
When a commit introduces a behavioural change — a new prompt, a model swap, a guardrail — it should be accompanied by evidence that the change does not degrade quality. The
footer links the commit to a specific eval run that was completed before merge.

This can be a URL to a CI run, a row ID in an internal eval database, or a reference to an artefact in the experiment tracker. The critical property is that it is durable: someone reading the commit in two years should be able to retrieve the original eval results.
— link to a row in the eval registry or a shared spreadsheet:
commits where no eval was run. Omitting the footer signals that the author judged the change non-behavioural.
A PR check can enforce that any commit with
in its footer also has an
footer, blocking merges where the author skipped the quality check.
Safety-critical changes deserve a second pair of eyes from someone accountable for the system's safety posture. The
footer records who reviewed and approved a
commits that touch the system prompt, jailbreak mitigations, or persona constraints.
commits when a new model version is known to have different refusal characteristics.
The value is a GitHub handle or an organisational role identifier:
The commit hook (shown above) enforces this footer's presence on

footer is defined by Conventional Commits to signal that a change is not backwards-compatible. In a traditional API, this means a function signature changed or a field was removed. In an agentic AI system, the definition is wider.
— the agent now returns a different JSON shape, removing a field or renaming a key that downstream systems consume. Downstream consumers break silently if they do not validate the schema.
— a tool that external callers invoke has had a parameter renamed, made mandatory, or removed. Callers using the old signature will receive errors or silently wrong results.
— a template variable that callers were expected to supply (e.g.
) has been removed from the prompt, but callers still pass it. Depending on the templating library, this may raise an error or produce garbled output.
— raising the required pass rate constitutes a breaking change for any pipeline that was passing at the old threshold. Teams that relied on the softer bar now fail CI.
— downgrading to a cheaper model that does not support a tool-calling format used by the orchestration layer is a breaking change even if the API surface looks the same.
When a breaking change is present, the footer must describe what breaks and how callers should migrate:
BREAKING CHANGE: output now omits 'sources' field; callers must check for undefined before rendering citation list.

The following examples show complete commit messages for each extended type. The subject line, body, and footers together provide the context a reviewer, changelog generator, or future debugger needs.

# 1. prompt — system prompt tightening
prompt(research-agent/system): require numbered footnotes for all citations

The agent was citing sources inline with parenthetical text which was
ambiguous when a sentence referenced multiple documents. This change
requires footnote-style citations ([1], [2]) with a numbered list at
the end of every response.

Change-Class: behavioural
Prompt-Hash: sha256:3f4c8a91e2b7
Eval-Run: https://ci.example.com/runs/5102
Safety-Reviewer: @alice

---

# 2. eval — new benchmark cases
eval(rag-accuracy): add 30 multi-hop question cases from domain review

The domain review identified a gap in multi-hop retrieval coverage.
30 new QA pairs have been added; expected accuracy is 78% on this
subset at current model settings.

Change-Class: structural
Refs: #312

---

# 3. safety — PII scrubber
safety(output-filter): add regex scrubber for UK National Insurance numbers

Red-team exercise REX-2024-08 found that the agent would echo NI
numbers present in retrieved documents. This filter strips strings
matching the NI format before the response is returned to the caller.

Change-Class: behavioural
Safety-Reviewer: @bob
Eval-Run: https://ci.example.com/runs/5089
Refs: REX-2024-08

---

# 4. model — provider migration
model(staging): pin to claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022

Migrating staging from the previous snapshot to align with the
production target for Q4. Tool-call format is compatible; verified
against the existing eval suite.

Change-Class: behavioural
Eval-Run: https://ci.example.com/runs/5115
BREAKING CHANGE: structured-output mode now returns 'type' field
  as a discriminated union; callers that matched the old string
  literal must update their type guards.

---

# 5. agent — parallel tool fan-out
agent(orchestration): execute independent tool calls in parallel

When the planner emits multiple tool calls with no data dependency
between them, they are now dispatched concurrently via asyncio.gather.
Median latency on multi-tool turns drops from 3.4 s to 1.1 s.

Change-Class: structural
Eval-Run: https://ci.example.com/runs/5121

---

# 6. perf — embedding cache
perf(vector-db): cache embedding lookups with 24-hour TTL

Semantic-cache hit rate in production is ~60% for repeated user
queries. Adding an in-memory LRU cache with a 24-hour TTL reduces
embedding API calls by roughly half. No change to retrieval results
because the underlying vectors are deterministic.

Change-Class: structural

is an interactive CLI that walks developers through writing a commit message via a series of prompts, eliminating the need to remember the format from scratch. It can be extended with a custom adapter that knows about the GenAI type set.
Install commitizen and a custom adapter:
function that presents the extended type list to the developer, asks for scope, subject, body, and the relevant optional footers. For
types, the adapter makes the body and reviewer fields mandatory in the interactive flow, surfacing requirements at authoring time rather than at hook time.
file to define the type list without writing a full adapter.
In a monorepo with multiple agents, the commitizen adapter can ask which agent the commit targets and auto-populate the scope.
Squash Merge Strategy and Commit Message Templates

Many teams squash-merge pull requests, collapsing a branch's work into a single commit on main. This means the branch-level commits — often informal work-in-progress messages — disappear from the main branch history. The squash commit message must carry all the semantic metadata of the entire branch.

When squashing a PR that includes prompt or safety changes, the PR title must itself be a valid conventional commit subject line. GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket all allow configuring a PR merge template that pre-populates the squash commit message. Configure the template to include placeholder lines for each footer field.
Set the PR title format rule
— require that PR titles follow the same conventional commit format as commit subjects. A GitHub Action can check this on every PR open event.
) with a "Commit footers" section that the author fills in before merge.
Run commitlint against the squash message
hook that runs locally can be run in CI against the squash commit message file before merge is permitted.
, individual commits land on main verbatim. In this strategy, each commit on the branch must already be properly typed; no squash step fixes poor messages.
Enforcement: PR Title Checks and Merge Gates
Convention without enforcement is only a suggestion. The following checks form a minimal enforcement layer for an agentic AI repo's merge pipeline.
A GitHub Actions workflow that runs on
events validates the PR title using commitlint. Because the PR title becomes the squash commit message subject, validating it here prevents a non-conforming commit from reaching main.
GitHub Action performs this check out of the box, configured with the extended type list via its
Make the check a required status check in branch protection rules so that a failing PR title blocks the merge button.
event (title edits) so that authors can fix the title without closing and reopening the PR.
A separate required check scans all commits in the PR for the presence of
and, if found, verifies that an
footer is also present. If a behavioural change has no corresponding eval run, the check fails with an explanatory message directing the author to run the eval suite and update the commit footer.
type commits, a merge gate verifies that the

footer names a user who has approved the PR on GitHub. This combines commit-message metadata with the code review approval record, creating a double-confirmation that someone with safety responsibilities signed off on the change.
Use a CODEOWNERS rule that maps the safety component directories to the safety team, ensuring the team is always requested as reviewers.
Configure branch protection to require one approval from a code owner for merges to
The CI gate script cross-checks the footer handle against the list of approvers returned by the GitHub API, failing the check if they do not match.
Keep the CODEOWNERS file updated as the safety team roster changes. A stale CODEOWNERS entry can leave
commits without a required reviewer, allowing them to slip through.

Standard Conventional Commits types are insufficient for agentic AI repos because prompt files, model configs, eval suites, and safety guardrails are first-class artefacts with no standard type counterpart.
) to carry AI-specific traceability metadata that standard footers cannot express.
and non-empty body as hard requirements for
hook; surface these requirements early rather than at PR review time.
broadly in AI systems: output schema changes, tool signature renames, prompt parameter removals, and model capability reductions all break downstream consumers even without a code-level API change.
Combine commitlint (author-time) with PR title checks and merge gates (CI-time) to create a multi-layer enforcement system that catches non-conforming commits before they reach main.

In squash-merge workflows, the PR title is the commit message; validate it with the same rules as individual commits and use a PR description template to prompt authors for the required footer fields.
